data.title: Clorox fined $14.15M for failing to report product contamination to consumer watchdog
data.description:
Clorox (CLX) has agreed to pay a $14.15M penalty to the U.S. Consumer Product Safety Commission ((CPSC)) for failing to immediately report a product defect with its Pine-Sol products.

In 2019, Clorox (CLX) scientists reported that storage tanks and finished Pine-Sol products may have contained Pseudomonas aeruginosa, an environmental organism found in soil and water that can cause serious infections when exposed to people with weakened immune systems or external medical devices.

Clorox subsequently took steps to mitigate the potential for bacterial contamination but did not immediately report the incident to the CPSC as required.

“Prompt reporting of safety risks saves lives, and CPSC will not hesitate to enforce the law when companies fail to meet their obligations,” said CPSC acting chairman Peter Feldman.

In addition to a $14.15M penalty, Clorox (CLX) must maintain internal controls and procedures to ensure compliance with the Consumer Product Safety Act and submit an annual report outlining its compliance program, internal controls, and audit of compliance training.

data.title: Home Bancorp outlines mid-single digit loan growth for 2026 while expanding Texas presence
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Home Bancorp, Inc. (HBCP) Q4 2025

Management View

  • John Bordelon, Chairman, President & CEO, highlighted, "Yesterday afternoon, we reported fourth quarter net income of $11.4 million or $1.46 per share. For the full year 2025, net income was $46 million or $5.87 per share which is a record for Home Bank and 29% higher than our 2024 earnings per share." He emphasized growth in deposits and strong expansion in the Texas market, noting, "Since entering the Texas market in 2022, loans have grown at a 15% annual rate and now represent 20% of our loan portfolio."
  • Bordelon also stated, "Our pipeline is building and paydowns appear to be slowing, so we expect growth in 2026 to be in the mid-single digits."
  • On credit quality, Bordelon reported, "Nonperforming loans increased in 2025, but our charge-offs remain very low, and we don't expect that to change due to our conservative underwriting standards and proactive credit management."
  • David Kirkley, Senior EVP & CFO, said, "Fourth quarter net income totaled $11.4 million, an 8% decrease from the prior quarter but a 21% increase from a year ago. The decline in net income was primarily due to an increase in provision expense related to loan growth during the quarter."
  • Kirkley noted, "Net interest income was stable when compared to third quarter, decreasing $58,000 while NIM decreased 4 basis points to 4.06%." He added, "Average deposits increased by $58 million in the fourth quarter and by $187 million or 7% in 2025."

Outlook

  • Management expects loan growth in 2026 to be in the "mid-single digits."
  • Kirkley projected, "We expect noninterest income to increase to between $3.8 million and $4 million over the next several quarters. Noninterest expenses are expected to be between $22.5 million and $23 million in the first quarter and then increase to between $23.3 million and $23.7 million from there."
  • Bordelon indicated optimism for the Texas franchise, expecting increased productivity from the lending team and a new full-service branch opening in Houston in Q1 2026.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter net income was $11.4 million.
  • Net interest margin for the quarter was 4.06%.
  • Return on assets was reported as 1.29% for the quarter.
  • Loans grew by $38 million in the quarter, or 6% annualized.
  • Average noninterest-bearing deposits, representing 27% of total deposits, increased by $3 million in the quarter.
  • The cost of interest-bearing deposits decreased 6 basis points in the quarter, and the overall cost of deposits was 1.84%.
  • Noninterest income for the quarter was $4 million, slightly above the expected range.
  • Noninterest expenses totaled $23 million.
  • Allowance for loan loss ratio was stable at 1.21%.

Q&A

  • Feddie Strickland, Hovde Group: Asked about the trajectory of Class 5 and NPAs. Bordelon responded, "It takes a little bit longer, especially those credits in Louisiana and Mississippi. Texas products typically move a little bit faster… we do believe that the two subdivision properties in Texas, we should be getting back out of foreclosure or they should be sold by February 3."
  • Strickland: Asked if NPAs could come down by $5.5 million. Bordelon replied, "We hope so. We think the property is if we do take them back should be able to sell relatively quickly."
  • Strickland: Inquired about loan pipeline composition. Bordelon said, "We did have payoffs and pay downs throughout our portfolio… we're thinking hopefully, we have less of that in 2026. So the loan growth is there if we don't have the payoffs."
  • Strickland: Asked about customer sentiment in different markets. Bordelon noted, "We're not hearing anything negative in any of our markets, especially with rates coming down…"
  • Joseph Yanchunis, Raymond James: Asked about SBA business growth. Bordelon stated, "We do anticipate that with the lower interest rates, that should pick up. I don't think we're low enough yet to where it's going to be tremendous, but it should be much better than it has been in the last two years."
  • Yanchunis: Asked about M&A as a capital deployment priority. Bordelon replied, "Now that our stock price is getting closer to a [ 140 ] of tangible or so, we feel as though we have the power to go out and maybe look for a little bit larger banks... we're very optimistic about 2026 M&A."
  • Yanchunis: Inquired about the bond portfolio size. Kirkley said, "I expect the investment portfolio to increase... by about $15 million to $20 million."
  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler: Asked about expanding the Texas team. Bordelon confirmed, "We're very excited about that team and hope to continue to grow in that market."
  • Scouten: Asked about loan growth capacity. Bordelon stated, "I still think based upon the pipeline that we had in fourth quarter, I think first half of the year is still going to be mid-single digits."
  • Scouten: Inquired about NIM trajectory. Kirkley explained, "We see NIM ticking back up to 4.1% and 4.15% throughout the year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns about credit quality, nonperforming assets, and deposit competition, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as they probed for clarity on credit developments and growth opportunities.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic stance, emphasizing strong credit performance, franchise growth, and proactive management. Bordelon's remarks, "We are confident in Home Bank's future and our ability to meet our higher standards in all economic climates," reflected this tone.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment remained consistent, while management's confidence was more pronounced due to record earnings and clear growth plans.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from expecting "more moderate growth of 1% to 2% in 2025" to anticipating "mid-single digit" loan growth for 2026.
  • Strategic focus increased on Texas expansion, with a new branch set to open and the Texas loan portfolio now representing 20% of total loans, up from previous mentions.
  • Analysts' focus moved from loan payoffs and moderating growth in Q3 to questions on growth drivers, M&A, and asset quality in Q4.
  • Key metrics shifted, with Q4 net income below Q3 due to higher provision expense, but annual results reached record highs.
  • Management's tone evolved from cautious optimism about rate cuts and loan growth to stronger confidence in pipeline strength and market positioning.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted increased nonperforming loans but stressed that "charge-offs remain very low" and are not expected to materially worsen due to conservative underwriting.
  • Property-specific risks were discussed, with management detailing strategies for resolving Texas foreclosure properties and maintaining strong collateral positions.
  • Competitive deposit pricing pressures persist, noted as a headwind to NIM expansion as some banks offer "really high CD rates."
  • Analyst concerns focused on credit quality, pace of NPA resolution, and the sustainability of loan growth amid a shifting rate environment.

Final Takeaway

Home Bancorp delivered record annual earnings for 2025 and expects to achieve mid-single digit loan growth in 2026, underpinned by a strengthening Texas franchise and proactive credit management. Management remains confident in its ability to outperform peers, sustain strong deposit growth, and pursue M&A opportunities as market conditions improve, while maintaining a disciplined approach to underwriting and risk management.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: First BanCorp. outlines 3–5% organic loan growth and 100% earnings return strategy for 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First BanCorp. (FBP) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Aurelio Alemán-Bermúdez, President, CEO & Director, began by stating, "Our results for this quarter represent a strong capstone to a year of outstanding performance and disciplined execution, highlighted by record revenues, positive operating leverage and stable credit performance." He highlighted $87 million in net income or $0.55 per share, a return on assets of 1.8%, and a 49% efficiency ratio for the quarter.
  • Alemán-Bermúdez noted growth in loans, specifically mentioning, "We continue to... deploy our capital to support our client by facilitating a $1.4 billion in loan origination during the quarter. Total loans grew by $80 million, mainly reflecting growth across the commercial segments." He also reported, "Core customer deposits increased by $267 million... we achieved this while proactively continuing to reduce total deposit costs."
  • On asset quality, he stated, "The ratio of nonperforming assets to total asset continued to decrease, reaching an all-time low level of 60 basis points during the quarter."
  • Alemán-Bermúdez announced an 11% increase to the quarterly common stock dividend to $0.20 per share starting in Q1 2026 and emphasized substantial capital returns, noting, "Since we began the buyback program in 2021, we have repurchased over 28% of shares outstanding."
  • Orlando Berges-González, Executive VP & CFO, reported, "we earned $87.1 million, $0.55 per share, which compares to the $100.5 million or $0.63 a share we had in the third quarter." He explained that last quarter included significant one-time items. "Adjusted pretax preprovision income was $129.2 million, which compares to $121.5 million in the third quarter."

Outlook

  • Alemán-Bermúdez provided guidance, stating, "Our guidance remains largely unchanged. We are focused on delivering 3% to 5% organic loan growth, sustaining 52% or better efficiency ratio, maintaining a strong profitability metrics and returning close to 100% of annual earnings back to shareholders."
  • Management expects asset quality to remain stable, with consumer credit quality gradually returning to pre-pandemic levels.
  • Berges-González added, "we expect that margin will grow 2 to 3 basis points per quarter during 2026."

Financial Results

  • Berges-González said, "Net interest income for the quarter... reaching $222.8 million." He highlighted, "Net interest margin for the quarter was 4.68%, but adjusted for these items would have been 4.65% or 8 basis points higher than last quarter."
  • Operating expenses for the quarter were $126.9 million, slightly higher than last quarter due to increased compensation and marketing, but the efficiency ratio improved to 49%.
  • The allowance for credit losses on loans increased by $2 million to $249 million, representing 1.9% of loans.
  • Net charge-offs for the quarter were $20.4 million or 63 basis points of average loans, in line with the prior quarter.
  • Tangible book value per share increased by 4% to $12.29.
  • The Board approved an $0.02 per share increase in the quarterly dividend, and management intends to continue share repurchases of approximately $50 million per quarter through 2026.

Q&A

  • Anya Pelshaw, Hovde Group, LLC, Research Division, asked about net interest margin levers and liquidity mix. Berges-González replied, "The levers would be similar... it's going to come from these cash flows on the investment portfolio... we see the loan pipeline on the commercial side and residential being really strong... not so much on the consumer side."
  • Pelshaw asked about competition and cost of funds. Berges-González noted, "We do have still some wholesale funding... Those are coming due and are being reissued... at lower rates."
  • Pelshaw asked about credit quality changes. Alemán-Bermúdez answered, "We believe there is stability... We saw some deterioration on the consumer delinquencies, which is normalized... we don't see potential disruptors."
  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division, inquired about the auto loan market and tariffs. Alemán-Bermúdez responded, "We saw the quarter... a contraction in the portfolio of about $6 million... we don't expect any growth in the segment, so... unless there is adjustments on tariff or excise tax."
  • Moss asked about securities cash flows and reinvestment. Berges-González said, "We're expecting that we can see somewhere between 2 and 3 basis points pickup on those cash flows... we'll continue to see agency investments."
  • Moss followed up on the telecom NPL. Alemán-Bermúdez responded, "There's not a lot of new information on it... there's a lot of value behind it... just waiting as we manage any other NPA towards resolution."
  • Moss asked about capital deployment and CET1 ratios. Alemán-Bermúdez stated, "Our priorities are to organic growth... we continue organic expansion in Florida... if nothing comes to the table that meets our accretion and value... we continue using the capital to continue deploying to shareholders buying back the shares."
  • An unknown analyst from KBW sought clarification on the efficiency ratio guidance. Berges-González confirmed, "The efficiency ratio is typically calculated with everything... on a GAAP basis, so that it's reported consistently."
  • The same analyst asked about noninterest-bearing deposit flows. Alemán-Bermúdez said, "That's the value of the franchise, and we have multiple initiatives always in place to achieve that... it's a core strategy that we put a lot of emphasis across all regions."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral, focusing on margin dynamics, loan growth, competitive environment, and asset quality, with no significant skepticism or pressing negativity.
  • Management maintained a confident and positive tone in both prepared remarks and responses, with statements like "We are in great capital position, continue to make the right investments to modernize and help our franchise to drive both growth and efficiencies and deliver strong performance in 2026." Responses to questions were detailed and direct, indicating confidence in the bank's outlook and strategy.
  • Compared to last quarter, both analyst and management sentiment remained stable, with management continuing to exude confidence and analysts primarily seeking clarification on outlook and execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 was provided, targeting 3% to 5% organic loan growth and a 52% or better efficiency ratio, compared to the prior quarter's guidance for year-end loan growth of 3% to 4% and similar efficiency focus.
  • Management reiterated the priority of returning close to 100% of annual earnings to shareholders and maintained a steady capital deployment strategy, mirroring the previous quarter but with the announcement of an 11% dividend increase.
  • Analysts' focus remained on margin trends, loan growth, deposit competition, and credit quality, with new attention on the sustainability of noninterest-bearing deposits and the specifics of the efficiency ratio calculation.
  • Key metrics such as net interest income and tangible book value per share increased, while net charge-offs and allowance for credit losses remained stable quarter over quarter.
  • Management's confidence and messaging were consistent with the previous quarter, emphasizing stable credit, disciplined growth, and proactive capital management.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged potential macroeconomic headwinds, including "tariff-related pricing, inflationary pressures and geopolitical tensions."
  • Alemán-Bermúdez flagged normalization in consumer credit and continued monitoring of the unsecured market.
  • Competitive deposit pricing and the impact of interest rate movements on funding costs and loan yields were discussed as ongoing risks.
  • Analysts raised questions about competition, loan growth, and credit quality, but management described strategies in place to address these concerns, including proactive deposit repricing and a focus on core customer relationships.

Final Takeaway

First BanCorp. closed 2025 with record financial results, disciplined expense control, and robust capital returns to shareholders. The company enters 2026 with clear guidance for 3% to 5% organic loan growth, a continued focus on efficiency, and a commitment to returning nearly all earnings to shareholders, supported by stable credit performance and targeted investments in both digital and branch channels. Management signaled ongoing vigilance regarding macroeconomic risks, but expressed confidence in the bank's ability to deliver consistent results and capitalize on growth opportunities in its core markets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Northwest Bancshares outlines $710M–$730M revenue target and organic growth strategy for 2026 following Penns Woods acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Northwest Bancshares, Inc. (NWBI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • President and CEO Louis Torchio emphasized the transformative impact of 2025, highlighting the successful acquisition and integration of Penns Woods, which brought Northwest into the top 100 U.S. banks by assets, expanded its Pennsylvania presence with 20 new financial centers, and added thousands of new customers. Torchio stated that "we closed on a significant acquisition, drove record revenue of $655 million for the full year and continue to expand the firm's net interest margin." He also outlined ongoing investments in talent, technology, and new financial centers, including the opening of the first new location since 2018 in the Indianapolis MSA and pending expansions in Columbus, Ohio.
  • Torchio reported strong commercial banking momentum, stating, "we continue to strengthen and diversify our commercial banking business with C&I momentum of 26% year-over-year average loan growth." He also noted the material growth in SBA lending, indicating Northwest earned a spot among the top 50 originators in the U.S. and closed significant funding for its Columbus-based business at year-end.
  • Torchio highlighted leadership investments, saying, "over the past 18 months, we've made significant investments in executive and regional leadership, hiring accomplished executives across consumer and commercial banking, wealth management, legal and finance." He noted that more than half of profits were returned to shareholders through a quarterly dividend of $0.20 per share, marking the 125th consecutive quarterly dividend.
  • Chief Financial Officer Douglas Schosser reported, "we delivered a strong fourth quarter, and we successfully completed all remaining merger conversion activities on time and on budget," and reviewed financial performance, emphasizing record revenue, improved net interest margin, and disciplined expense management.

Outlook

  • Schosser provided full year 2026 guidance, stating, "we expect to see loan growth in 2026 in the low to mid-single digits and deposit growth in the low single digits. We expect revenues to be in the range of $710 million to $730 million and net interest margin in the low 3.70s." He also projected noninterest income between $125 million and $130 million, noninterest expense of $420 million to $430 million, net charge-offs between 20 to 27 basis points, and a flat tax rate of approximately 23%.
  • Management indicated that 100% of merger-related cost savings are expected to be realized in Q1 2026, ahead of schedule, and these are "fully reflected in our outlook."

Financial Results

  • Schosser reported GAAP EPS for the quarter of $0.31 per share and adjusted EPS of $0.33 per share, compared to $0.29 per share in the prior quarter. Net interest income grew $6.2 million or 4.6% quarter-over-quarter, with net interest margin increasing to 3.69%. Noninterest income increased by $5.5 million or 17% over the prior quarter, driven by higher bank-owned life insurance income. Total revenue increased $11.8 million quarter-over-quarter.
  • Pretax pre-provision net revenue rose to $66.4 million, a 92% increase from the third quarter on a GAAP basis. Adjusted efficiency ratio improved to 59.5% in Q4, up 10 basis points quarter-over-quarter.
  • Average loans grew $414 million quarter-over-quarter, and end-of-period loans increased by $66 million, ending the year at $13 billion. Loan yield rose to 5.65%, and average commercial loans increased $162 million quarter-over-quarter.
  • Average total deposits were up $475 million, and customer nonbrokered deposits rose $507 million over the prior quarter. Cost of deposits decreased 2 basis points to 1.53%.
  • Securities portfolio purchases totaled $363 million, with portfolio yield increasing 29 basis points to 3.11%. Noninterest income was up $5.6 million quarter-over-quarter but down $2.3 million year-over-year due to one-time gains in the prior year.
  • Adjusted efficiency ratio was 59.5%, with higher compensation and benefits in Q4 due to the full quarter impact of Penns Woods operations and increased performance-based incentives.
  • ACL coverage decreased to 1.15%, with annualized net charge-offs at 40 basis points due to a $9.2 million student housing loan charge-off. Total delinquencies increased, but 90-day plus delinquencies and NPAs both declined quarter-over-quarter.

Q&A

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson, asked about expense trends and seasonality; Schosser confirmed that Q1 may see higher expenses due to FICA resets and merit increases but expects subsequent quarters to be lower. Rulis inquired about performance-based costs in Q4, and Schosser affirmed some nonrecurring year-end items contributed to elevated figures. On net interest margin guidance, Schosser said it includes purchase accounting accretion and assumes two more rate cuts in 2026, but "our NIM guidance really isn't contingent on those 2 rate cuts."
  • Timothy Switzer, KBW, asked about the net purchase accounting impact to NII; Schosser stated, "the $4 million and the 4 basis points was effectively what we were kind of going back and recalculating all of that to." Switzer also probed about SBA business growth; Schosser and Torchio described a balanced approach to retaining and selling SBA loans, with a focus on prudent scaling. Switzer further asked about talent in the SBA business, and Torchio said, "we've gone to the best and the best of the industry and recruited from those franchise."
  • Timothy DeLacey, Raymond James, queried about securities portfolio timing and future growth; Schosser explained purchases occurred in October and November and the goal is to keep the portfolio at about 17% of assets. On CRE, Schosser said opportunities exist, but growth will be "flat to slight growth" over the next year or two.
  • Kyle Gierman, Hovde Group, asked about loan growth segments; Schosser reported broad-based growth, especially in national verticals, and noted that "the pipeline is looking very good." He described payoffs as normal, with a focus shifting to organic business post-merger.
  • Matthew Breese, Stephens Inc., requested details on the BOLI death benefit, which Schosser confirmed as "about $6.5 million." Breese asked about CD repricing; Schosser sees a "10 to 15 basis point opportunity" as CDs reprice lower. On core deposit costs, Schosser believes reductions will lag rate decreases by 30 to 45 days.
  • Breese asked about M&A appetite; Torchio responded, "we're open to conversations, nothing imminent for us," emphasizing organic growth focus for 2026.
  • Manuel Navas, Piper Sandler, asked about NIM guidance progression and charge-off assumptions; Schosser anticipates a "slightly improving margin" later in the year and expects charge-offs to remain steady. On loan growth mix, Schosser and Torchio described balanced growth across consumer and commercial segments, targeting a roughly 50/50 mix.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, focusing on the sustainability of expense controls, the trajectory of NIM, and growth opportunities, with probing questions about one-time items and strategic priorities.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone throughout, especially in prepared remarks, using phrases such as "I'm confident in our trajectory" and "we really like the flexibility that this provides for us." In Q&A, responses remained measured and factual, with no notable defensiveness.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts appeared more focused on forward-looking growth and integration benefits, while management's confidence increased, with fewer caveats regarding merger-related risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management guidance evolved from reaffirming Q4 2025 targets in the previous quarter to providing comprehensive 2026 guidance, including revenue, margin, and efficiency goals.
  • Strategic focus shifted from merger execution and integration in Q3 to organic growth, network expansion, and scaling specialized lending verticals in Q4.
  • Analysts in Q4 focused more on the drivers of sustainable earnings growth, NIM progression, and cost savings realization, compared to prior concerns about merger impacts and credit quality.
  • Key financial metrics showed sequential improvement: adjusted EPS rose to $0.33 from $0.29, net interest margin increased to 3.69% from 3.65%, and efficiency ratio improved by 10 basis points.
  • Management's tone became more assertive about growth and less focused on integration-related risks.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited net charge-offs elevated by a $9.2 million student housing loan but clarified there is "no meaningful concentration in student housing" in the current portfolio.
  • Total delinquencies rose primarily due to mortgage loans, but 90-day plus delinquencies and NPAs declined. Management reiterated focus on reducing classified assets and highlighted continued improvement within the CRE portfolio.
  • Analysts raised questions about potential expense seasonality, deposit cost pressures, and the sustainability of growth in new and existing markets.
  • Management plans to mitigate risks by maintaining tight expense controls, prudent scaling of new lending verticals, and ongoing portfolio diversification.

Final Takeaway

Northwest Bancshares management underscored a transition from integration to organic growth following a successful acquisition year, setting forth a 2026 outlook that targets $710 million to $730 million in revenue, low to mid-single digit loan growth, and improved efficiency. With the full realization of merger-related cost savings ahead of schedule and continued expansion into new markets, leadership expressed strong confidence in the company’s trajectory, balanced loan portfolio, and their ability to drive shareholder value through disciplined execution and diversified business lines.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gemini to shut down Nifty Gateway platform amid NFT slump
data.description:
Crypto exchange Gemini Trust (GEMI) is shuttering its digital-art-token marketplace, Nifty Gateway, as the once booming non-fungible token ("NFT") space continues to reel from a drop in prices and trading activity in recent years, according to a recent statement.

The NFT marketplace, launched in 2020, will close on Feb. 23, 2026.

Nifty Gateway users with USD or ETH (ETH-USD) balances and NFTs will receive instructions on transferring their assets to Gemini's (GEMI) broader platform, as the company shifts toward building a "one-stop super app," it said in the statement.

The company owned by the Tyler and Cameron Winklevoss twins will keep supporting NFTs through the Gemini Wallet, which was rolled out in August 2025.

GEMI shares slid 5.3% in afternoon trading as crypto prices struggled for direction.

data.title: Omeros higher after first commercial sales of transplant therapy
data.description:
• Shares of Omeros Corporation (OMER) gained on Tuesday after the company announced first commercial shipments of Yartemlea, its recently approved therapy for hematopoietic stem cell transplant-associated thrombotic microangiopathy, a complication of stem cell transplantation.
  • The Seattle, Washington-based biotech received FDA approval in December to market Yartemlea for patients aged two years and above with TA-TMA. A condition linked to hematopoietic stem cell transplantation, TA-TMA, has a more than 90% mortality rate in severe cases.
  • Omeros (OMER) stated that the first commercial shipments of Yartemlea began last week, and concurrently, it fulfilled initial orders for the drug received from multiple transplant centers.
  • Adults and children with TA-TMA are now undergoing Yartemlea therapy, including those who have failed prior off-label C5-inhibitor regimens in both inpatient and outpatient-based care settings, the company added.

data.title: Tesla to install semi chargers at Pilot travel centers along major trucking routes
data.description:
• Tesla (TSLA) has inked a deal with Pilot Travel Centers to install heavy-duty electric truck charging stations at certain Pilot stations along major trucking routes in California, Georgia, Nevada, New Mexico, and Texas, where the need for charging is highest.
  • “Heavy-duty charging is yet another extension of our exploration into alternative fuel offerings, and we’re happy to partner with a leader in the space that provides turnkey solutions and deploys them quickly,” said Shannon Sturgil, senior vice president of alternative fuels at Pilot.
  • The charging stations will initially only accommodate Tesla (TSLA) semis but may be expanded in the future to be compatible with other heavy-duty EVs.
  • Construction on the charging network will begin in the first half of 2026 and is expected to be ready for use by this summer.
  • With more than 900 travel centers in 44 U.S. states and six Canadian provinces, Pilot has the largest network of travel centers in North America.

data.title: Cloudflare soars as AI chatbot running on its infrastructure goes viral
data.description:
Cloudflare (NET) shares had jumped 12% by noon trading on Tuesday, following reports of an AI chatbot running on Cloudflare's infrastructure that had gone viral.

This followed Cloudflare's 10% run-up during Monday trading.

The AI assistant, originally named Clawdbot, was renamed to Moltbot on Tuesday after Anthropic (ANTHRO) asked the bot's creator, Peter Steinberger, to change the name due to potential trademark issues, according to Forbes.

Moltbot is an open-source AI agent built on Anthropic's Claude models. Its connection to Cloudflare stems from the fact that as these agents scale, they require secure and low-latency infrastructure. Cloudflare's global edge network provides this necessary support.

"We believe Cloudflare's price surge is being caused by increasing awareness of Cloudflare's role in AI infrastructure due to social media discussions related to an open-source AI assistant built on Anthropic's Claude model, Clawdbot," said TD Cowen analyst Shaul Eyal in an investor note.

"The focus of the interest is Cloudflare Tunnel, which allows developers to connect locally run tools such as AI agents on the internet without exposing their servers," Eyal added.

Moltbot functions as a digital helper. It can perform tasks such as managing a user's inbox and calendar, drafting messages, checking travelers in for flights, and writing code, all with minimal prodding.

It can run within a wide array of applications, such as Meta's (META) WhatsApp, Discord (DSCD), Salesforce's (CRM) Slack, Microsoft (MSFT) Teams, Apple's (AAPL) iMessage, Spotify (SPOT), Canvas, OpenAI's (OPENAI) ChatGPT, xAI's (X.AI) Grok, and dozens more.

data.title: Lam Research leans on AI tailwinds as China weighs on outlook
data.description:
Lam Research (LRCX) is scheduled to announce Q2 earnings results on Wednesday, January 28th, after market close.

The consensus EPS Estimate is $1.17 and the consensus revenue estimate is $5.24B.

Over the last 2 years, LRCX has beaten EPS estimates 100% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 100% of the time.

Over the last 3 months, EPS estimates have seen 2 upward revisions and 1 downward. Revenue estimates have seen 2 upward revisions and 1 downward.

On ratings, Lam Research holds a Buy consensus on Wall Street, with an average price target of $215.35, implying about 9.8% downside. In contrast, the Seeking Alpha Quant model rates the stock as Hold, citing a stretched valuation. LRCX's stock momentum is strong, with a one-year price performance of 215.3%, far above the sector median.

In October, Lam Research (LRCX) posted strong Q1 results, with revenue up 27.6% year over year to $5.32B and EPS of $1.26, beating expectations on both revenue and earnings.

While this performance remains a positive driver, management signaled slower growth ahead. For Q2, Lam guided revenue to about $5.2B ± $300M, implying roughly 21% year-over-year growth at the midpoint, down from 27% in Q1. The primary cause for this slowdown is cited to be the Chinese end market.

Bullish view

“Lam Research benefits from a razor-and-blades model, generating high-margin, recurring service revenue that mitigates cyclicality and enhances profitability. Major tailwinds include surging AI compute demand, rising process intensity, advanced packaging adoption, and memory chip shortages driving capacity expansion,” SA analyst Cedric Pfort said.

Cautious view

“The recently implemented 50% affiliate rule as well as the correction of the overordering from the Chinese market in 2023-2024 should pose a considerable headwind in the coming few quarters in the company's revenue, especially given its substantial (30-40% of total revenue) revenue contribution,” explains SA analyst Alpha Mantra. As per management, they expect ~$200M and $600M revenue impact from these headwinds in 2QFY26 and full-year FY26, respectively.

data.title: Alcoa downgraded at Morgan Stanley after strong YTD performance
data.description:
Alcoa (AA) +1.6% in Tuesday's trading despite receiving a downgrade to Equal Weight from Overweight with a $64 price target at Morgan Stanley, which sees a more balanced risk-reward after the stock significantly outperformed peers in recent weeks, up nearly 50% since December 1.

Alcoa (AA) is taking several actions to increase productivity, reduce costs and optimize its asset portfolio, and the company will be receiving IRA production tax credits for U.S. aluminum production of $50M-$60M/year, Morgan Stanley analyst Carlos De Alba said.

A tighter aluminum market should keep profitability elevated while U.S. regional premiums are now reflecting a 50% tariff, and the company has identified 10 high-priority curtailed sites that it is actively marketing to hyperscalers for data center conversion; however, the stock's strong YTD outperformance leaves the stock with a more balanced risk-reward, the analyst said.

While Alcoa (AA) should continue to benefit from elevated London Metal Exchange aluminum pricing and a U.S. Midwest permium that recently broke the $1/lb level, these factors are now properly reflected in the company's share price, according to De Alba.

Also, the timeline for a potential transaction to transform a curtailed site into a data center continues to get pushed back, and the recent escalation in trade tensions between the U.S. and Canada suggests a potential Section 232 exemption for Canadian aluminum may be more difficult, the analyst said.
